primary_diagnosis.name: Chronic scalp psoriasis, mild, acutely excoriated and fissured (no secondary infection)

pathophysiology_summary: Psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated hyperproliferative dermatosis in which accelerated epidermal turnover produces thick, adherent silvery scale on well-demarcated erythematous plaques, characteristically at the scalp margin/anterior hairline. Vigorous mechanical scratching of pruritic plaques breaches the fragile, hypervascular epidermis (dilated dermal capillaries close to the surface — the basis of Auspitz sign), causing the pinpoint bleeding and small haemorrhagic crusts seen here. Harsh detergent/anti-dandruff shampoos strip the skin barrier and act as irritants, aggravating the itch-scratch cycle and the acute burning flare. The alarming features (burning, bleeding, crusting) are therefore a mechanically excoriated flare of a benign chronic plaque disease, NOT a spreading soft-tissue infection: there is no honey-coloured crusting (impetigo), no indurated spreading erythema or fluctuance (cellulitis/abscess), no regional lymphadenopathy and no systemic upset. Topical corticosteroid reduces the underlying inflammation and, with removal of the irritant and cessation of scratching, the flare settles.

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic relapsing flaky red scalp with thick silvery scale at the hairline, now acutely itchy and excoriated after an irritant — the classic picture.
Bleeding, crusting and burning raise the fear of infection; must be actively screened because it changes management to antimicrobial +/- urgent review.

differential.diagnosis: Allergic or irritant contact dermatitis to hair products
differential.evidence_basis: New shampoo preceding the flare is a red herring pointing to contact dermatitis; irritant effect is real but the morphology is psoriatic.
The classic misdiagnosis of hairline scaling; distinguished by greasy yellow scale and less sharply demarcated borders versus thick silvery well-demarcated psoriatic plaque.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, infection must sit on the list as the important-not-to-miss item even though the leading diagnosis is a benign flare; the test is holding both and screening infection out before reassuring.

differential.diagnosis: Scalp psoriasis flare with excoriation, no secondary infection
differential.evidence_basis: No spreading indurated erythema, no honey-coloured crust, no fluctuance, no lymphadenopathy, afebrile — infection screened negative on available (largely history/video) evidence.
Cannot be FULLY excluded via telehealth (no palpation for fluctuance/induration, no lymph node exam); remains a safety-net trigger rather than a current diagnosis.
Bilateral, symmetric plaque distribution along the hairline rather than a unilateral dermatomal vesicular eruption.
Sharply demarcated silvery hyperkeratotic scale argues for psoriasis over a diffuse weeping eczematous reaction, but the irritant contribution warrants ceasing the product.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct move after screening is confident-but-not-certain: name the benign flare, cease the irritant, treat topically, AND arrange timely in-person review because remote exam cannot fully exclude early infection.

differential.diagnosis: Chronic scalp psoriasis, mild, acutely excoriated (no secondary infection)
differential.evidence_basis: Well-demarcated silvery-scaled hairline plaques with excoriation on a chronic background, no infective or systemic features; responds to topical corticosteroid and irritant removal.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading indurated erythema extending off the plaques onto the forehead/face
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would signal cellulitis/erysipelas of the face and scalp, a soft-tissue infection needing urgent antibiotics and possible admission.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated facial cellulitis can progress to abscess, orbital/periorbital involvement or systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Thick honey-coloured crusting or purulent weeping discharge
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate impetiginisation (secondary bacterial infection), changing management to topical/oral antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Spreading impetigo and, in scratch-seeded skin, deeper infection.

red_flags.description: Fever, chills or feeling systemically unwell
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic upset with a skin infection raises the possibility of invasive/spreading infection requiring emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early sepsis from a soft-tissue source.

red_flags.description: Plaques becoming hot, tensely swollen or fluctuant
red_flags.clinical_significance: Heat/fluctuance suggests localised infection or abscess — assessment requires palpation the AI cannot perform.

red_flags.description: Sudden unexplained hair loss in the affected areas (scarring)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Could indicate scarring alopecia or a more aggressive scalp process warranting dermatology review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Permanent scarring hair loss if a scarring process is not identified early.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the dramatic complaint ('on fire', bleeding, terror of infection) and escalating to ED for IV antibiotics for what is a benign excoriated flare.
Prescribing systemic antibiotics for bleeding/crusting that is mechanical excoriation, not infection — an error of commission with no infective evidence.
Calling silvery well-demarcated hairline plaques 'just dandruff / seborrhoeic dermatitis' and under-treating the underlying psoriasis.
Fixating on the new shampoo and diagnosing pure allergic contact dermatitis, missing the chronic psoriatic morphology beneath the irritant flare.
Confidently excluding cellulitis/impetigo remotely without palpation or lymph-node exam, and failing to arrange in-person review or infection safety-netting.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for fluctuance, induration or warmth of the plaques
Definitive close inspection to exclude subtle honey-coloured crusting or early cellulitis
Measured temperature and vital signs without a home device
Skin swab / bacterial culture if infection were suspected
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a benign excoriated psoriasis flare, cease the irritant shampoo, recommend a topical corticosteroid scalp preparation, and arrange timely in-person GP review (within 24-48h) to lay eyes on the scalp and exclude secondary infection — with clear infection safety-netting to ED if spreading redness, pus, fever or hot swelling develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The risk here is NOT under-diagnosis of an emergency but two opposite errors: over-triage/over-treatment (ED + IV antibiotics) driven by the alarming story, and false reassurance (declaring 'definitely not infected' remotely). The calibrated answer treats the flare while keeping the infection exit-ramp open.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Topical corticosteroids (e.g. betamethasone valerate scalp application) are first-line for localised scalp psoriasis; avoid irritant products; consider tar/salicylic-acid preparations for scale.
Potent topical corticosteroid to the scalp for flares; step down to maintenance; escalate/refer if extensive, refractory, or diagnostic uncertainty.
